nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs and adjacent sandy/rubble zones in shallow to moderately deep water. Hides under live rock, in crevices, and within reef rubble during the day; emerges at night to forage. Often associated with reef slopes, lagoonal patch reefs, and seagrass-edge habitats.

decorPreferences: ample mature live rock with deep crevices and overhangs for daytime hiding
sandy or fine-rubble substrate where the brittle star can shelter and forage
rockwork arranged to create caves and shaded refuges away from strong direct lighting
stable, established reef system (generally advised tank age of at least 6 months) with consistent water chemistry
secure rockwork that will not topple as the star moves between hides at night

diet.notes: Opportunistic carnivore and scavenger that has been documented actively hunting live prey in addition to scavenging detritus and uneaten food. In captivity, target-feed several times a week with meaty foods such as chopped shrimp, mussel, clam, silversides, marine fish flesh, and sinking carnivore pellets, delivered directly to the substrate near the star's hide. Underfed specimens are reported to become more aggressive toward fish and shrimp tankmates, so a generous, consistent feeding schedule is generally advised.

goodWith: medium-to-large active fish that swim above the substrate (tangs, larger wrasses, larger angelfish)
established LPS and soft corals (generally considered reef-safe toward sessile invertebrates)
robust hermit crabs and larger snails
mature reef systems with abundant rockwork and stable parameters
avoidWith: small or slow-moving fish (cardinalfish, firefish, dartfish, small gobies, blennies, dragonets, seahorses, pipefish)
sleeping reef fish that rest on or near the substrate at night
ornamental shrimp (Lysmata, Stenopus, sexy shrimp, anemone shrimp) — frequently taken as prey
other Green Brittle Stars in smaller systems (intraspecific aggression has been reported)
any system that has been treated with copper-based medications
newly cycled or unstable tanks with fluctuating salinity or pH

summary: FISH-PREDATION WARNING. A large Indo-Pacific brittle star widely sold as a reef detritivore but documented to actively ambush and eat small fish and ornamental shrimp at night using an umbrella-trap hunting tactic. Reef-safe toward corals, but not safe with small or slow tankmates.
careNotes:
FISH-PREDATION CAVEAT (read first): Ophiarachna incrassata is one of the few brittle stars in the trade with a well-documented reputation as an active predator of live fish, not merely a scavenger. Multiple aquarium sources describe an 'umbrella' ambush tactic in which the star props its central disc up on its arms to create a cave-like void above the substrate ('pseudocrevice'), then drops down to enwrap any small fish or shrimp that ventures underneath seeking shelter at night. Reported prey includes cardinalfish, small gobies, firefish, dartfish, blennies, dragonets, and ornamental shrimp (Lysmata, Stenopus, sexy shrimp, anemone shrimp). The nickname 'the green death' is in common hobby use for this reason. This behavior is reported to be more pronounced in underfed specimens; generous, consistent target feeding several times per week is the most commonly advised mitigation, but the risk to small/slow tankmates is never zero. Generally considered reef-safe toward corals and other sessile invertebrates — coralSafe is set true — but the species should NOT be added to tanks housing small fish or ornamental shrimp that it can ambush.

Other husbandry notes: a large, fast-growing brittle star (commonly 30–50 cm arm-tip to arm-tip at adult size) that needs a mature, established reef system with stable parameters and abundant rockwork for daytime hiding. Sensitive to abrupt salinity, temperature, and pH swings — slow drip-acclimation over at least 30–60 minutes is widely advised. Like all echinoderms it is intolerant of copper-based medications. Care level is rated 'easy' to 'moderate' across sources (BlueZoo: beginner; Reeflings: moderate-to-easy); intermediate is written here as the averaged value, reflecting the fact that physical husbandry is straightforward but tankmate selection is unforgiving. Source spread on adult size was wide — LiveAquaria/BlueZoo report 18 in (~45 cm), Reef Builders ~20 in (~50 cm), Reeflings 30 cm, Quality Marine ~23 cm leg length — averaged here as 30–50 cm. Min-tank recommendations spread from Reeflings 100 L to BlueZoo/Reef Builders 50 gal (~190 L); 200 L is written as the cautious nice-round floor, justified by the large adult span and the need for ample rockwork to dissuade fish-ambush behavior.
breedingNotes: Not known to have been bred in captivity. Like most ophiuroids, Ophiarachna incrassata is expected to spawn gametes into the water column with planktonic larval development that is impractical to rear in standard reef systems. No documented hobbyist breeding protocol exists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary. The specific product-page URL returned 404 at the time of research; species-specific data was extracted via BlueZoo Aquatics (a comparable trade-source primary) and cross-checked against the secondaries below.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary. The site returned 403 Forbidden at the time of research; documented as a failed lookup per the playbook fallback rule. Replaced with Reef Builders, Reeflings, Quality Marine, and Tank Facts as effective secondaries.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics product page
additional.notes: Effective primary trade source. Indo-Pacific/Bali origin, adult size 18 in (~45 cm), 50 gal (~190 L) minimum tank, care level beginner, temperament semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, carnivorous diet (mussel, clam, raw table shrimp, silversides), explicit note on the species' reputation for catching gobies and small slow-moving fish.

additional.name: Reeflings species care guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Ophiodermatidae, Ophiurida), adult size 30 cm, min tank 100 L, temperature 24–28 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, care level moderate-to-easy, reef-safe, peaceful-with-caveat temperament, predation on small fish/inverts if underfed.

additional.name: Reef Builders — 'The Green Brittle Star: Little Fishy, Beware!'
additional.notes: Behavioral source documenting the umbrella-ambush hunting tactic in detail (props on arms, suspended oral disc, 'sprung trap' on prey venturing beneath). Reports ~20 in arm-tip-to-arm-tip adult span. Origin of the 'green death' nickname.

additional.name: Quality Marine — 'The Interesting Incrassata'
additional.notes: Cross-checked range (worldwide tropical seas except Atlantic), shallow-reef habitat (<30 ft), 'pseudocrevice' hunting behavior, prey range (small fish, shrimp, molting crustaceans), preference for decorative clams in larger specimens. Additional water-parameter confirmation also obtained from Tank Facts (https://www.tankfacts.com/invertebrates/saltwater/starfish/green-brittle-star_314): 24–27 °C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, 8–12 dKH.
